*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: mcmc_baseline.py
@@
-    raise NotImplementedError("MCMC sampler has not been implemented yet.")
+    if num_samples <= 0:
+        return ([], 0.0)
+    if proposal_std <= 0:
+        raise ValueError("proposal_std must be positive.")
+
+    samples = []
+    accepted = 0
+
+    current_x = float(start_val)
+    current_log_p = target_log_pdf(current_x)
+
+    for _ in range(num_samples):
+        proposal_x = random.gauss(current_x, proposal_std)
+        proposal_log_p = target_log_pdf(proposal_x)
+
+        # log acceptance ratio: log(P(x') / P(x)) = logP(x') - logP(x)
+        log_ratio = proposal_log_p - current_log_p
+        log_alpha = min(0.0, log_ratio)
+
+        # Accept if log(u) < log(alpha), where u ~ Uniform(0, 1)
+        # Entirely in log-space for numerical stability.
+        if math.log(random.random()) < log_alpha:
+            current_x = proposal_x
+            current_log_p = proposal_log_p
+            accepted += 1
+
+        samples.append(current_x)
+
+    acceptance_rate = accepted / num_samples
+    return (samples, acceptance_rate)
*** End Patch

input_source.note: This input was selected based on the immediately preceding workflow context.

observation: The agent had gathered current Ontario job-posting rules, resignation case law, retention requirements, and the user’s factual timeline.
output:
**Short answer:** yes. The client has two main risk areas: Brian’s attempted rescission of a heat-of-the-moment resignation, and Ontario’s 2026 public job-posting requirements.

**1. Brian’s Resignation**
Under Ontario law, a resignation must be clear and unequivocal, assessed contextually. The Ontario Court of Appeal has emphasized that surrounding circumstances matter, and that an employee may resile from a resignation if the employer has not relied on it to its detriment: [Kieran v. Ingram Micro Inc.](https://www.minicounsel.ca/oca/2004/4852), paras. 30, 34. Later cases confirm the same contextual approach: [English v. Manulife](https://www.minicounsel.ca/oca/2019/612), paras. 21-26, and [Nagpal v. IBM](https://www.minicounsel.ca/onca/2021/274), paras. 43-44, 57-59.

Brian’s case is mixed, but risky for the employer. Saying “I quit,” returning the laptop/keycard, leaving, and not working Friday afternoon are strong objective evidence of resignation. But the resignation happened during a heated dispute, and Brian returned at 8:30 a.m. on the next business day, before doing any work, apologized, and expressly rescinded. May 9 and May 10 were weekend days, so the President’s statement that there had been “no contact…for multiple days” is technically true but may overstate the practical absence.

The company’s “detrimental reliance” argument is weak on these facts. By 9:30 a.m. on May 11, the company had prepared a draft posting and the President had told HR at 9:15 a.m. it could be posted. But the posting was not actually public until 9:57 a.m., after Brian tried to rescind. No replacement had been hired, no offer made, and no public search had yet started when Brian met the President. Weekend HR time is some reliance, but likely not the kind of material detrimental reliance that would justify refusing a prompt rescission by a senior, long-service employee.

So the client faces a meaningful wrongful dismissal risk if it treats Brian as having resigned. A court could find either that the resignation was not sufficiently unequivocal in context, or that Brian validly rescinded before the employer materially relied on it.

**2. Posting The CFO Role**
Because the posting went public on May 11, 2026, Ontario’s new ESA job-posting rules were in force. For employers with 25+ employees, a publicly advertised job posting must include compensation information, AI-use disclosure, and whether an existing vacancy exists; it must not include Canadian-experience requirements. See Ontario’s ESA guide and O. Reg. 476/24: [Ontario job-posting requirements](https://www.ontario.ca/document/your-guide-employment-standards-act-0/requirements-related-publicly-advertised-job), [O. Reg. 476/24](https://www.ontario.ca/laws/regulation/240476).

Issues with this posting:

- **AI disclosure:** The company planned to use an AI candidate sorting system to highlight candidates meeting the interview threshold. That is very likely AI used to “screen, assess or select” applicants. The posting, as described, did not disclose AI use. That is likely non-compliant.

- **Vacancy statement:** The posting had to state whether it was for an existing vacancy. The described posting does not include that statement. Non-compliant. If Brian’s rescission was legally effective, saying there was an existing vacancy would also be problematic.

- **Compensation:** “Commensurate with experience and competitive with industry standards” likely does not satisfy the expected compensation/range requirement unless the CFO role’s expected compensation is over $200,000 annually or the top of the range exceeds $200,000, in which case the compensation disclosure requirement does not apply. For a large-company CFO that exemption may well apply, but the client should document the expected compensation basis.

- **Canadian experience:** The preference for 3+ years as a senior executive at another company in the automotive space is not, on its face, a Canadian-experience requirement, so this part appears acceptable.

- **Records:** The company must retain copies of the posting and associated application forms for three years after public access is removed. Ontario’s ESA also requires retention of interview-decision information where applicants are interviewed.

**Bottom line:** The safest assessment is that the company acted too quickly. It should have paused the posting when Brian appeared on May 11, considered his rescission before publicizing the role, and ensured the posting complied with the 2026 ESA rules. The biggest employment-liability risk is wrongful dismissal; the clearest statutory posting issue is failure to disclose AI use and vacancy status.
reasoning: The collected authorities supported a concise risk assessment: resignation rescission created wrongful-dismissal exposure, and the CFO posting likely violated new ESA public-posting requirements.

Identify and explain any issues in the following contractual clause based on its wording and the additional considerations unique to the matter that are outlined after the clause has been provided. After any issues have been identified and explained, re-draft each problematic portion of the clause to address the issue. Ensure that proper legal drafting techniques are applied when editing any portion of an existing clause, including:

i. match the writing style of the existing document;
ii. edit as little of the original language as possible, while also ensuring that an issue is thoroughly addressed;
iii. apply defined terms as appropriate; and
iv. add defined terms when necessary.

If there is anything that is unclear, whether that be a potential issue with the language of the included contractual clause or a concern related to how the clause should be edited, identify the concern and note that additional information or context may resolve the issue.

The following is the clause that is to be reviewed:

“13.01 While any Shareholder owns Shares of the Corporation, or if any Shareholder sells or transfers the Shares owned by them, as the case may be (in such a case the respective Shareholder shall be referred to as the "Selling Shareholder"), then for a period of eighteen (18) months thereafter within the Territory (as hereinafter defined), the Selling Shareholder shall not:
1. (i) directly or indirectly engage in, control, advise, manage, serve as a director, office, employee of, act as consultant to, receive any economic benefit from or exert any influence on any business which conducts activities or a business which is competitive with the Corporation’s Business;
2. (ii) employ, solicit for employment, or encourage to leave the employment, any Person who was during the twelve (12) month period prior to such employment, solicitation or encouragement, an officer or employee of the Corporation;
3. (iii) disturb or attempt to disturb any business relationship between any third party and the Corporation; and
4. (iv) make any statement to any third party, including the press or media, likely to result in adverse publicity for the Corporation.

Section 13.01 shall not apply to clients of a Shareholder who was a client of a Shareholder prior to the date herein.

Section 13.01 shall apply to all shareholders of a Shareholder which is a corporation.

13.02 Territory
Territory is defined as being one hundred and fifty kilometers from the property municipally known as [Corporation’s registered office address]."

Additional considerations unique to this matter:
- Jurisdiction: Ontario, Canada.
- Type of agreement: Shareholders’ Agreement
- Type of clause in question: Non-compete and non-solicitation clause
- Corporate structure: Owned in equal parts by two individual shareholders, who are each a director and officer of the corporation.
- Business: The corporation is a holding company that owns and operates a construction business that operates primarily in Toronto and the GTA, but their operations also extend to southwestern and central Ontario.
- Business size: Small or medium sized business.
- My client: One of the shareholders. They are asking for the agreement to be reviewed prior to rolling their shares in the OpCo into this newly created corporation.
- Outlook: The business is established. The shareholders expect it to grow, but not substantially and the territory that it services is likely not to expand any further. Each shareholder has a family and is unlikely to leave Toronto or the surrounding area. There is room for an additional minority investor, and the share structure has been created in a way that would accommodate such an expansion, but there are no immediate plans in that regard.
- Identity of drafter: The original agreement was drafted by counsel to the corporation.
- Material defined terms: The term “Business” is defined elsewhere in the agreement to mean “any business then carried on by the Corporation in the province of Ontario.” All other defined terms used in the clause are either defined within the clause (e.g “Selling Shareholder”) or carry standard definitions (e.g. “Shareholder” means a shareholder of the Corporation.).
- Share transfer restrictions: The agreement contains standard restrictions on share transfers (i.e. ROFR, shotgun clause, pre-emptive right) and a list of standard permitted transfers (i.e. transfers to a trust or corporation that is controlled by an existing shareholder).

input:
Below is a drafting review, not a legal opinion. Ontario enforceability will depend on the full agreement, consideration, the parties’ roles, and the factual record. The key legal backdrop is that Ontario’s ESA generally prohibits employer/employee non-competes entered into on or after October 25, 2021, subject to limited exceptions, including executives. Even where the ESA does not void the covenant, Canadian common law still requires restrictive covenants to be clear, reasonable in time, geography, and activity, and no broader than necessary.

**Issues And Redrafts**

**1. The opening language is grammatically unclear and may not identify who is bound**

Problem: The clause says “While any Shareholder owns Shares… or if any Shareholder sells or transfers…” and then refers only to the “Selling Shareholder”. If a Shareholder still owns Shares, there may be no “Selling Shareholder”. The phrase “for a period of eighteen (18) months thereafter” is also unclear: thereafter from ownership, sale, transfer, or cessation of ownership?

This also matters because a permitted transfer to a holding company, family trust, or controlled corporation could technically trigger the restriction even though the transfer is internal and the original shareholder remains involved.

Suggested redraft:

> 13.01 While any Shareholder owns Shares of the Corporation and, if such Shareholder ceases to own any Shares of the Corporation other than by way of a Permitted Transfer, for a period of eighteen (18) months thereafter, within the Territory, such Shareholder, and any Shareholder who ceases to own any Shares of the Corporation shall not:

If “Permitted Transfer” is not already defined, add:

> “Permitted Transfer” means a transfer of Shares permitted under this Agreement to a trust, corporation or other Person controlled by a Shareholder, provided that such Shareholder continues to control such transferee.

**2. The non-compete is likely overbroad**

Problem: Subsection 13.01(i) prohibits the Selling Shareholder from engaging in, advising, managing, being employed by, consulting to, receiving “any economic benefit from”, or exerting “any influence on” any business competitive with the Corporation’s Business. That is very broad. “Receive any economic benefit from” could capture passive investments, diversified funds, retirement accounts, or immaterial interests. “Exert any influence” is vague. “Any business which conducts activities or a business which is competitive” is awkward and potentially broader than necessary.

Because the Corporation is a holding company, there is also a business-definition issue. If “Business” means only business carried on by the Corporation, and the operating construction business is carried on through an OpCo or subsidiary, the restriction may miss the intended business unless the defined term already captures subsidiaries.

Suggested redraft:

> (i) directly or indirectly engage in, control, advise, manage, serve as a director, officer or employee of, or act as consultant to, any business that is competitive with the Business, other than as a passive holder of not more than five percent (5%) of the outstanding shares of a corporation whose shares are listed on a recognized stock exchange;

If the defined term “Business” does not include the operating construction business and subsidiaries, consider adding:

> “Business” means any business then carried on by the Corporation, directly or indirectly through any subsidiary, in the province of Ontario.

**3. Ontario ESA risk should be addressed**

Problem: Both equal shareholders are also directors and officers. If they are also employees, the non-compete may be attacked under Ontario’s ESA unless an exception applies. The “executive” exception may apply only if the individual holds a qualifying executive office, such as president, CEO, COO, CFO, or another chief executive position. Merely being a director or officer may not be enough.

The clause cannot fully draft around statutory unenforceability, but it can reduce avoidable ambiguity.

Suggested addition, if accurate:

> For greater certainty, each Shareholder acknowledges that such Shareholder is entering into this Section 13.01 in such Shareholder’s capacity as a shareholder, director and officer of the Corporation, and not solely in such Shareholder’s capacity as an employee of the Corporation.

If the shareholders hold specific executive titles, the agreement should identify those titles elsewhere. More context is needed before relying on this approach.

**4. Employee non-solicit lookback is awkward**

Problem: Subsection 13.01(ii) prohibits solicitation of any Person who “was during the twelve (12) month period prior to such employment, solicitation or encouragement” an officer or employee. The phrase “prior to such employment” is confusing because it appears to refer to employment by the restricted party. It may also capture former employees who left long before the restricted act.

Suggested redraft:

> (ii) employ, solicit for employment, or encourage to leave the employment of the Corporation, any Person who is then, or was during the twelve (12) month period prior thereto, an officer or employee of the Corporation;

**5. “Disturb any business relationship” is vague and too broad**

Problem: Subsection 13.01(iii) could capture ordinary competition, relationships with suppliers, lenders, landlords, consultants, or unrelated third parties. “Disturb” is imprecise. A court may find it too vague to enforce.

Suggested redraft:

> (iii) solicit, interfere with, or attempt to interfere with, any business relationship between the Corporation and any customer, client, supplier or subcontractor of the Corporation with whom the Shareholder had material dealings during the twelve (12) month period prior thereto;

**6. The publicity restriction is too broad**

Problem: Subsection 13.01(iv) prohibits any statement “likely to result in adverse publicity”. This could capture truthful statements, legally required disclosures, regulatory complaints, litigation filings, or protected communications.

Suggested redraft:

> (iv) make any false, misleading or disparaging statement to any third party, including the press or media, concerning the Corporation or its Business, except where required by law.

**7. Client carve-out is unclear and grammatically defective**

Problem: “clients of a Shareholder who was a client of a Shareholder prior to the date herein” is unclear. It should say whether the carve-out applies to clients of the shareholder personally, clients of the shareholder’s separate business, or clients brought into the Corporation. It also should identify the relevant date.

Suggested redraft:

> Section 13.01 shall not apply to any client of a Shareholder who was a client of such Shareholder prior to the date of this Agreement, provided that such client did not become a client of the Corporation after the date of this Agreement.

If the intention is to allow the shareholder to continue serving pre-existing clients even if they later become Corporation clients, that needs different drafting.

**8. Corporate shareholder extension is overbroad**

Problem: “Section 13.01 shall apply to all shareholders of a Shareholder which is a corporation” is too broad. It could bind passive minority owners of a corporate shareholder, including a future minority investor’s shareholders. It may also be difficult to enforce unless those persons are parties or sign joinders.

Suggested redraft:

> Section 13.01 shall apply to each individual who directly or indirectly controls a Shareholder which is a corporation, and each such individual shall execute a counterpart or joinder to this Agreement agreeing to be bound by this Section 13.01.

**9. Territory may be underinclusive or arbitrary**

Problem: “one hundred and fifty kilometers from the property municipally known as [registered office address]” may not match the business’s actual operating area. The business operates mainly in Toronto/GTA but also in southwestern and central Ontario. A 150 km radius from the registered office may exclude some actual service areas and include areas where the Corporation does not operate. Courts dislike arbitrary geography in restrictive covenants.

Suggested redraft:

> Territory is defined as the City of Toronto, the Greater Toronto Area, and any municipality in southwestern or central Ontario in which the Corporation carried on the Business during the twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the applicable date of determination.

If certainty is preferred over a floating definition, list the municipalities or regions.

**10. Typographical issue**

Problem: “serve as a director, office, employee of” should be “director, officer or employee of”.

Suggested redraft is included in item 2 above.

**Consolidated Revised Clause**

> 13.01 While any Shareholder owns Shares of the Corporation and, if such Shareholder ceases to own any Shares of the Corporation other than by way of a Permitted Transfer, for a period of eighteen (18) months thereafter, within the Territory, such Shareholder, and any Shareholder who ceases to own any Shares of the Corporation shall not:
>
> (i) directly or indirectly engage in, control, advise, manage, serve as a director, officer or employee of, or act as consultant to, any business that is competitive with the Business, other than as a passive holder of not more than five percent (5%) of the outstanding shares of a corporation whose shares are listed on a recognized stock exchange;
>
> (ii) employ, solicit for employment, or encourage to leave the employment of the Corporation, any Person who is then, or was during the twelve (12) month period prior thereto, an officer or employee of the Corporation;
>
> (iii) solicit, interfere with, or attempt to interfere with, any business relationship between the Corporation and any customer, client, supplier or subcontractor of the Corporation with whom the Shareholder had material dealings during the twelve (12) month period prior thereto; and
>
> (iv) make any false, misleading or disparaging statement to any third party, including the press or media, concerning the Corporation or its Business, except where required by law.
>
> Section 13.01 shall not apply to any client of a Shareholder who was a client of such Shareholder prior to the date of this Agreement, provided that such client did not become a client of the Corporation after the date of this Agreement.
>
> Section 13.01 shall apply to each individual who directly or indirectly controls a Shareholder which is a corporation, and each such individual shall execute a counterpart or joinder to this Agreement agreeing to be bound by this Section 13.01.
>
> 13.02 Territory  
> Territory is defined as the City of Toronto, the Greater Toronto Area, and any municipality in southwestern or central Ontario in which the Corporation carried on the Business during the twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the applicable date of determination.

Key unresolved points: whether the shareholders hold ESA “executive” titles, whether the Corporation directly carries on the operating construction business or does so through subsidiaries/OpCo, and whether pre-existing client relationships are meant to remain entirely outside the Corporation’s restrictive covenant regime.
